patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] Demotion cleanup and fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves functions related to demoting memory usage in the Linux kernel from mm/vmscan.c to mm/memory-tiers.c, as per Alexandre Ghiti's suggestion. The goal is to centralize these functions in a single file for better organization and maintainability. No functional changes are intended by this move.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn suggested moving a helpful comment from vmscan.c to memory-tiers.c, noting that it clarifies the behavior of folios in @demote_folios when not demoted.

Reviewer noted that mt_demote_folios() function is not properly protected by locks and may lead to data corruption or other issues, but the rest of the patch looks good.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alexandre,

I hope you are doing well! Thank you for the patch.

Makes sense to move the migration functions together. Just one small
nit, I think the following comment is pretty helpful in understanding
that folios that aren't demoted still remain in @demote_folios. Should
we also move this comment to memory-tiers.c?

[...snip...]

---

[...snip...]

Anyways, the rest looks good to me. Have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: Rename node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function node_get_allowed_targets() to make its purpose more explicit, specifically indicating that it returns tier nodes targeted during demotion.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Approved the renaming of node_get_allowed_targets() to node_get_allowed_demotion_targets(), suggesting a shorter name might be more suitable but not considering it a significant issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 12:02:41 +0100 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:

> This function actually returns the tier nodes that are targeted during a
> demotion, so rename it to be more explicit.
> 
> No functional change intended.

Agreed, node_get_allowed_targets is pretty vague ; -)

I do think that node_get_allowed_demotion_targets could be considered
a bit too long, but I don't think it's called in too many places where
there would be more than just this function call in the line so LGTM!

Please feel free to add my review tag, have a great day Alexandre!

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a bug in the migration code where GFP_RECLAIM is cleared after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE, but it should be done before. The fix involves clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE to ensure consistency with regular THP allocations.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: The patch looks good and addresses the issue correctly. The fix is straightforward and should be applied.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a demotion gfp issue by preserving the initial gfp reclaim policy when allocating migration targets. The problem arises when src folios are hugetlb pages, causing htlb_modify_alloc_mask() to unconditionally enable reclaim. To fix this, the patch introduces a new variable gfp_entry to preserve the initial gfp flags.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach to preserving initial gfp flags. Suggested an alternative solution using a different variable to store the modified gfp mask.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages across nodes. The patch also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and adds calls to create node heads for cgroups when splittable pages are created. Additionally, it introduces helpers to the list_lru API for caller-side locking. The deferred split queue lock is replaced with a more efficient approach using list_lru's locking mechanisms.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Conditional memcg awareness was introduced to zs_pool, addressing concerns about storing unnecessary values in every pool.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Identified unnecessary storage of values in every pool and suggested a cleaner approach using a struct with enum members.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and suggested improvements to the function prototype and variable names.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Agreed to implement the suggested changes and thanked Johannes for his review.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 16:16:34 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51:40PM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> > Introduce 3 new fields to struct zs_pool to allow individual zpools to
> > be "memcg-aware": memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat.
> > 
> > memcg_aware is used in later patches to determine whether memory
> > should be allocated to keep track of per-compresed object objgs.
> > compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat are enum indices that point into
> > memcg (node) stats that zsmalloc will account towards.
> > 
> > In reality, these fields help distinguish between the two users of
> > zsmalloc, zswap and zram. The enum indices compressed_stat and
> > uncompressed_stat are parametrized to minimize zswap-specific hardcoding
> > in zsmalloc.
> > 
> > Suggested-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> > ---
> >  drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c |  3 ++-
> >  include/linux/zsmalloc.h      |  5 ++++-
> >  mm/zsmalloc.c                 | 13 ++++++++++++-
> >  mm/zswap.c                    |  3 ++-
> >  4 files changed, 20 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> > 
> > diff --git a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > index bca33403fc8b..d1eae5c20df7 100644
> > --- a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > +++ b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > @@ -1980,7 +1980,8 @@ static bool zram_meta_alloc(struct zram *zram, u64 disksize)
> >  	if (!zram->table)
> >  		return false;
> >  
> > -	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name);
> > +	/* zram does not support memcg accounting */
> > +	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name, false, 0, 0);

Hello Johannes,

Thank you for your review! I hope you are doing well : -)

> It's a bit awkward that 0 is valid (MEMCG_SWAP). Plus you store these
> values in every pool, even though they're always the same for all
> zswap pools.

Agreed. Originally I thought of removing memcg_aware and doing a bitwise
OR check of the two stats, but thought this was also a bit strange
(also because 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item anyways)

> How about:
> 
> /* zsmalloc.h */
> struct zs_memcg_params {
> 	enum memcg_stat_item compressed;
> 	enum memcg_stat_item uncompressed;
> };
> struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name, struct zs_memcg_params *memcg_params);
> 
> /* zswap.c */
> static struct zs_memcg_params zswap_memcg_params = {
> 	.compressed = MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
> 	.uncompressed = MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> };
> 
> then pass &zswap_memcg_params from zswap and NULL from zram.
> 
> > @@ -2071,6 +2079,9 @@ struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name)
> >  	rwlock_init(&pool->lock);
> >  	atomic_set(&pool->compaction_in_progress, 0);
> >  
> > +	pool->memcg_aware = memcg_aware;
> > +	pool->compressed_stat = compressed_stat;
> > +	pool->uncompressed_stat = uncompressed_stat;
> 
> 	pool->memcg_params = memcg_params;
> 
> And then use if (pool->memcg_params) to gate in zsmalloc.c.

These definitely look a lot cleaner. Will make these changes in v3!

Thanks again. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/11] mm/zsmalloc: Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the zs_object_copy function to zs_obj_copy, conforming to a naming pattern in zsmalloc functions.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> All the zsmalloc functions that operate on a zsmalloc object (encoded
> location values) are named "zs_obj_xxx", except for zs_object_copy.
>
> Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy to conform to the pattern.
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> All the zsmalloc functions that operate on a zsmalloc object (encoded
> location values) are named "zs_obj_xxx", except for zs_object_copy.
>
> Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy to conform to the pattern.
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> ---
>  mm/zsmalloc.c | 4 ++--
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> index 2c1430bf8d57..7a9b8f55d529 100644
> --- a/mm/zsmalloc.c
> +++ b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> @@ -1416,7 +1416,7 @@ void zs_free(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle)
>  }
>  EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(zs_free);
>
> -static void zs_object_copy(struct size_class *class, unsigned long dst,
> +static void zs_obj_copy(struct size_class *class, unsigned long dst,
>                                 unsigned long src)

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces conditional memory cgroup (memcg) awareness to the zsmalloc pool, allowing individual pools to be configured as memcg-aware or not. It adds three new fields to the struct zs_pool: memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat, which are used to determine whether memory should be allocated for per-compressed object accounting and what statistics to account towards. The patch also updates the zram driver to create a pool that is not memcg-aware. This change aims to minimize hardcoding specific to zswap in zsmalloc by parametrizing the enum indices for compressed and uncompressed stats.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce objcgs pointer in struct zpdesc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an array of obj_cgroup pointers to the zpdesc structure in the Linux kernel's zsmalloc module. The purpose is to track the memory group ownership (memcg) of compressed objects. This change increases the size of struct zpdesc by 8 bytes, but since it overlays a page structure, this increase remains invisible outside. However, functions that check for memcg data may be affected and need to use a specific bit to tag the pointer correctly. The patch also temporarily increases memory usage in zswap due to duplicated pointers, but future patches will redirect charging operations and remove the extra pointer. This change is guarded under CONFIG_MEMCG and can optionally be limited to CONFIG_MEMCG && CONFIG_ZSWAP for minimal impact on zram users.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zsmalloc layer to handle object cgroup (objcg) charging and lifetime management, aligning it with how memory is consumed in the zswap compression backend. The change accounts for the size of the slot occupied by each compressed object, rather than just its size, to provide a more accurate representation of memory usage. This brings the charging mechanism closer to accurately tracking memory consumption in the zpdesc. The patch modifies several files, including memcontrol.h and mm/memcontrol.c, to implement these changes.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that zsmalloc should not update zswap stats, and suggested moving these updates to the zswap code instead, citing potential future changes in zram's memcg charging support.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a need to handle the swapoff path and dropping of per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), but did not provide a clear plan for addressing this issue.

Author acknowledged that the clean logical divide between zsmalloc and zswap/zram leads to oversimplification of memory charging logic, missing accurate accounting for compressed objects' movements across physical nodes, and incorrect restriction of cpusets.mems during migration. Author is considering alternative designs, including adding backpointers from compressed objects to their corresponding zswap_entries or making zsmalloc call a hook into zswap code during migrations.
sentiment_signals: did not provide a clear plan
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Yosry, I hope you are doing well!
Thank you for your feedback : -)

---

Yeah... I think this was also a big point of concern for me. While reading
the code, I was really amazed by how clean the logical divide between
zsmalloc and zswap / zram were, and I wanted to preserve it as much as
possible.

There are a few problems, though. Probably the biggest is that migration
of zpdescs and compressed objects within them are invisible to zswap.
Of course, this is by design, but this leads to two problems.

zswap's ignorance of compressed objects' movements across physical nodes
makes it impossible to accurately charge and uncharge from the correct
memcg-lruvec.

Conversely, zsmalloc's ignorance of memcg association makes it impossible
to correctly restrict cpusets.mems during migration.

So the clean logical divide makes a lot of sense for separating the
high-level cgroup association, compression, etc. from the physical
location of the memory and migration / zpdesc compaction, but it would
appear that this comes at a cost of oversimplifying the logic and missing
out on accurate memory charging and a unified source of truth for the
counters.

The last thing I wanted to note was that I agree that zsmalloc doing
explicit zswap stat updates feels a bit awkward. The reason I chose to do
this right now is because when enlightening zsmalloc about the compressed
objs' objcgs, zswap is the only one that does this memory accounting.
So having an objcg is a bit of a proxy to understand that the consumer
is zswap (as opposed to zram). Of course, if zram starts to do memcg
accounting as well, we'll have to start doing some other checks to
see if the compresed object should be accounted as zram or zswap.

OK. That's all the defense I have for my design : -) Now for thinking
about other designs:

I also explored whether it makes sense to make zsmalloc call a hook into
zswap code during and after migrations. The problem is that there isn't
a good way to do the compressed object --> zswap entry lookup, and this
still doesn't solve the issue of zsmalloc migrating compressed objects
without checking whether that object can live on another node.

Maybe one possible approach is to turn the array of objcgs into an array
of backpointers from compressed objects to their corresponding zswap_entries?
One concern is that this does add 8 bytes of additional overhead per 
zswap entry, and I'm not sure that this is acceptable. I'll keep thinking
on whether there's a creative way to save some memory here, though...

Of course the other concern is what this will look like for zram users.
I guess it can be done similarly to what is done here, and only allocate
the array of pointers when called in from zswap.

Anyways, thank you for bringing this up. What do you think about the
options we have here? I hope that I've motivated why we want
per-memcg-lruvec accounting as well. Please let me know if there is anything
I can provide additional context for : -)

Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that changing the zswap stats is not feasible now because they were added before zsmalloc became a backend, and requested to maintain the existing stats.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that using separate zsmalloc pools for each objcg is sufficient and questioned the necessity of sharing a pool.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed expressed confusion about the patch's intention to switch to zsmalloc-based statistics, citing potential breakage of userspace applications.
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree, but we cannot change the zswap stats now that we added them.
Keep in mind that when they were added zsmalloc was not the only
backend.

---

It is distinguishable as long as they use different zsmalloc pools, I
don't see why we'd need to share a pool.

---

Not sure what you mean here? Changing the stats to be zsmalloc-based?
IIUC we can't do this without breaking userspace.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham requested clarification on their own previous comment, stating they were confused and needed more caffeine.
review_comments.raw_body:
No I meant your proposal haha. Sorry for being confusing - probably
need more caffeine :)

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm/memcontrol: Track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the memory controller to track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes, enabling sub-PAGE_SIZE granularity charging for compressed objects stored at the boundary of two zspages. The change involves updating various data structures and functions to use the new byte-based tracking.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the need for a new field in the memory controller data structure and suggested using an existing one instead. Also questioned the necessity of updating multiple functions to use the new byte-based tracking.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the tracking of ZSWAP_B and ZSWAPPED_B statistics to be per-memcg-lruvec, allowing for more accurate accounting of compressed object charges. The changes involve moving these statistics from node stats to memcg-lruvec stats and decoupling their updates.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the inaccuracy of NR_ZSWAPPED_B due to its coarse granularity and suggested that it should be kept accurate. However, acknowledged that invasive changes across all vmstat infrastructure would be required to fix this.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting by adding a new array of objcg pointers to the zpdesc structure, allowing for accurate tracking of memory usage by zswap and reducing NR_ZSWAP. The changes also move memcg charges to the zsmalloc layer, which is the only user of zswap at this time.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the increased size of struct zpdesc and the potential impact on performance. Suggested exploring alternative solutions to achieve the desired accounting functionality without incurring significant overhead.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Identified unnecessary storage of values in every pool and suggested a cleaner approach using a struct with enum members.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and suggested improvements to the function prototype and variable names.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Agreed to implement the suggested changes and thanked Johannes for his review.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 16:16:34 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51:40PM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> > Introduce 3 new fields to struct zs_pool to allow individual zpools to
> > be "memcg-aware": memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat.
> > 
> > memcg_aware is used in later patches to determine whether memory
> > should be allocated to keep track of per-compresed object objgs.
> > compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat are enum indices that point into
> > memcg (node) stats that zsmalloc will account towards.
> > 
> > In reality, these fields help distinguish between the two users of
> > zsmalloc, zswap and zram. The enum indices compressed_stat and
> > uncompressed_stat are parametrized to minimize zswap-specific hardcoding
> > in zsmalloc.
> > 
> > Suggested-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> > ---
> >  drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c |  3 ++-
> >  include/linux/zsmalloc.h      |  5 ++++-
> >  mm/zsmalloc.c                 | 13 ++++++++++++-
> >  mm/zswap.c                    |  3 ++-
> >  4 files changed, 20 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> > 
> > diff --git a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > index bca33403fc8b..d1eae5c20df7 100644
> > --- a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > +++ b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > @@ -1980,7 +1980,8 @@ static bool zram_meta_alloc(struct zram *zram, u64 disksize)
> >  	if (!zram->table)
> >  		return false;
> >  
> > -	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name);
> > +	/* zram does not support memcg accounting */
> > +	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name, false, 0, 0);

Hello Johannes,

Thank you for your review! I hope you are doing well : -)

> It's a bit awkward that 0 is valid (MEMCG_SWAP). Plus you store these
> values in every pool, even though they're always the same for all
> zswap pools.

Agreed. Originally I thought of removing memcg_aware and doing a bitwise
OR check of the two stats, but thought this was also a bit strange
(also because 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item anyways)

> How about:
> 
> /* zsmalloc.h */
> struct zs_memcg_params {
> 	enum memcg_stat_item compressed;
> 	enum memcg_stat_item uncompressed;
> };
> struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name, struct zs_memcg_params *memcg_params);
> 
> /* zswap.c */
> static struct zs_memcg_params zswap_memcg_params = {
> 	.compressed = MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
> 	.uncompressed = MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> };
> 
> then pass &zswap_memcg_params from zswap and NULL from zram.
> 
> > @@ -2071,6 +2079,9 @@ struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name)
> >  	rwlock_init(&pool->lock);
> >  	atomic_set(&pool->compaction_in_progress, 0);
> >  
> > +	pool->memcg_aware = memcg_aware;
> > +	pool->compressed_stat = compressed_stat;
> > +	pool->uncompressed_stat = uncompressed_stat;
> 
> 	pool->memcg_params = memcg_params;
> 
> And then use if (pool->memcg_params) to gate in zsmalloc.c.

These definitely look a lot cleaner. Will make these changes in v3!

Thanks again. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series aims to clean up and fix demotion-related functions in the Linux kernel's memory management code. It involves moving related functions together, renaming some functions for better clarity, and fixing GFP (General-Purpose Flags) handling issues.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Approved the move of migration functions together and suggested moving a helpful comment to memory-tiers.c. Also reviewed and approved the renaming of node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 12:02:40 +0100 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:

> Let's have all the demotion functions in this file, no functional
> change intended.

Hi Alexandre,

I hope you are doing well! Thank you for the patch.

Makes sense to move the migration functions together. Just one small
nit, I think the following comment is pretty helpful in understanding
that folios that aren't demoted still remain in @demote_folios. Should
we also move this comment to memory-tiers.c?

[...snip...]

> -/*
> - * Take folios on @demote_folios and attempt to demote them to another node.
> - * Folios which are not demoted are left on @demote_folios.
> - */
> -static unsigned int demote_folio_list(struct list_head *demote_folios,
> -				      struct pglist_data *pgdat,
> -				      struct mem_cgroup *memcg)
> -{
> -	int target_nid;
> -	unsigned int nr_succeeded;
> -	nodemask_t allowed_mask;
> -

[...snip...]

Anyways, the rest looks good to me. Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Approved the renaming of node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit, but suggested considering a shorter name for node_get_allowed_demotion_targets().
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 12:02:41 +0100 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:

> This function actually returns the tier nodes that are targeted during a
> demotion, so rename it to be more explicit.
> 
> No functional change intended.

Agreed, node_get_allowed_targets is pretty vague ; -)

I do think that node_get_allowed_demotion_targets could be considered
a bit too long, but I don't think it's called in too many places where
there would be more than just this function call in the line so LGTM!

Please feel free to add my review tag, have a great day Alexandre!

Reviewed-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  include/linux/memory-tiers.h | 6 +++---
>  mm/memory-tiers.c            | 4 ++--
>  mm/vmscan.c                  | 2 +-
>  3 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the stats exposed via /proc/vmstat are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not CONFIG_MEMCG, and suggested using mod_node_page_state to make global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello JP!

The stats are exposed via /proc/vmstat and are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not
CONFIG_MEMCG. Early returning overhere would make it inaccuate. Does
it make sense to use mod_node_page_state if memcg is not available,
so that these global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to track page allocations per mempolicy, but the author suggests a simpler approach by reusing existing logic for !CONFIG_MEMCG.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Proposed reusing existing logic for !CONFIG_MEMCG to simplify the patch.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Identified unnecessary storage of values in every pool and suggested a cleaner approach using a struct with enum members.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and suggested improvements to the function prototype and variable names.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Agreed to implement the suggested changes and thanked Johannes for his review.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 16:16:34 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51:40PM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> > Introduce 3 new fields to struct zs_pool to allow individual zpools to
> > be "memcg-aware": memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat.
> > 
> > memcg_aware is used in later patches to determine whether memory
> > should be allocated to keep track of per-compresed object objgs.
> > compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat are enum indices that point into
> > memcg (node) stats that zsmalloc will account towards.
> > 
> > In reality, these fields help distinguish between the two users of
> > zsmalloc, zswap and zram. The enum indices compressed_stat and
> > uncompressed_stat are parametrized to minimize zswap-specific hardcoding
> > in zsmalloc.
> > 
> > Suggested-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> > ---
> >  drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c |  3 ++-
> >  include/linux/zsmalloc.h      |  5 ++++-
> >  mm/zsmalloc.c                 | 13 ++++++++++++-
> >  mm/zswap.c                    |  3 ++-
> >  4 files changed, 20 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> > 
> > diff --git a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > index bca33403fc8b..d1eae5c20df7 100644
> > --- a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > +++ b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > @@ -1980,7 +1980,8 @@ static bool zram_meta_alloc(struct zram *zram, u64 disksize)
> >  	if (!zram->table)
> >  		return false;
> >  
> > -	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name);
> > +	/* zram does not support memcg accounting */
> > +	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name, false, 0, 0);

Hello Johannes,

Thank you for your review! I hope you are doing well : -)

> It's a bit awkward that 0 is valid (MEMCG_SWAP). Plus you store these
> values in every pool, even though they're always the same for all
> zswap pools.

Agreed. Originally I thought of removing memcg_aware and doing a bitwise
OR check of the two stats, but thought this was also a bit strange
(also because 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item anyways)

> How about:
> 
> /* zsmalloc.h */
> struct zs_memcg_params {
> 	enum memcg_stat_item compressed;
> 	enum memcg_stat_item uncompressed;
> };
> struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name, struct zs_memcg_params *memcg_params);
> 
> /* zswap.c */
> static struct zs_memcg_params zswap_memcg_params = {
> 	.compressed = MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
> 	.uncompressed = MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> };
> 
> then pass &zswap_memcg_params from zswap and NULL from zram.
> 
> > @@ -2071,6 +2079,9 @@ struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name)
> >  	rwlock_init(&pool->lock);
> >  	atomic_set(&pool->compaction_in_progress, 0);
> >  
> > +	pool->memcg_aware = memcg_aware;
> > +	pool->compressed_stat = compressed_stat;
> > +	pool->uncompressed_stat = uncompressed_stat;
> 
> 	pool->memcg_params = memcg_params;
> 
> And then use if (pool->memcg_params) to gate in zsmalloc.c.

These definitely look a lot cleaner. Will make these changes in v3!

Thanks again. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] cgroup: improve cgroup_file_notify() scalability

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] cgroup: reduce cgroup_file_kn_lock hold time in cgroup_file_notify()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces the hold time of cgroup_file_kn_lock in cgroup_file_notify() by taking a kernfs_get() reference under the lock and calling kernfs_notify() after dropping it, following the pattern from cgroup_file_show(). This change aims to improve performance by minimizing the global spinlock's hold time.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] cgroup: add lockless fast-path checks to cgroup_file_notify()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds lockless fast-path checks to cgroup_file_notify() to improve performance by reducing unnecessary lock acquisitions. The patch introduces two READ_ONCE() checks: one for a NULL kn pointer and another for the notified_at timestamp within a specific interval. If the timer is pending, it returns immediately without acquiring the lock.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with timer arming and rate limiting being moved out of lock. Suggested re-examining the locking mechanism to ensure it's correct.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] cgroup: replace global cgroup_file_kn_lock with per-cgroup_file lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series replaces the global cgroup_file_kn_lock with a per-cgroup_file spinlock to eliminate cross-cgroup contention and improve performance.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's impact on existing code, specifically the removal of the global cgroup_file_kn_lock. Suggested that the patch should not be merged without addressing these issues.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the scalability of cgroup_file_notify() by reducing lock hold time, adding lockless fast-path checks, and replacing a global spinlock with per-cgroup_file locks.

review_comments.author: Jakub Kicinski
review_comments.summary: Reported spinlock contention in the rx path of cgroup_file_notify() after a specific commit, leading to the development of this patch series.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Posted a follow-up reply to address the changes since v1, specifically moving rate-limiting code out of the lock.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC] Reimagining Memory Cgroup (memcg_ext)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The thread discusses the relevance and feasibility of implementing proactive reclaim in memory cgroups, specifically addressing concerns about attribution of consumed CPU cycles and preventing high-priority threads from runaway memory consumption.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about attribution of consumed CPU cycles and preventing high-priority threads from runaway memory consumption. Suggested that proactive reclaim is still worthwhile despite these challenges, but requires identifying reasonable and predictable semantics for allocating liability to participating groups.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC] Reimagining Memory Cgroup (memcg_ext)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages across nodes. The patch also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and adds calls to create node heads for cgroups when splittable pages are created. Additionally, it introduces helpers to the list_lru API for caller-side locking. The deferred split queue lock is replaced with a more efficient approach using list_lru's locking mechanisms.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: migrate: transfer large_rmappable flag in folio_migrate_flags()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a migration issue where the large_rmappable flag is not transferred from the source folio to the destination folio during migration, leading to use-after-free errors when the deferred split queue is processed. The problem occurs because migration allocators directly allocate folios without wrapping them in page_rmappable_folio(), which sets the large_rmappable flag. The patch transfers the large_rmappable flag in folio_migrate_flags() consistently with other folio flags, resolving the issue.

compaction_alloc_noprof() does the page_rmappable_folio() at the end.

It's a responsibility of the folio allocation code, not folio migration
code.

Cheers,

David

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
[PATCH v2] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's migration code where destination folios were not being requeued on the deferred split queue after a successful migration. The source folio is checked to see if it was on the deferred split queue before moving it, and if so, the destination folio is requeued with its partially mapped state. This ensures that underutilized THPs do not escape the shrinker after NUMA migration.
